questions.question: Why is data deletion in distributed systems usually more than a single database operation?
options: Because data often exists in caches, indexes, archives, and workflows beyond the primary record.
Because storage engines do not support delete statements.
Because deletion only matters for legal teams and not architecture.
Because replicas automatically delete data before primaries do.
questions.explanation: Real deletion semantics must cover every place the data is represented or served.

questions.question: What is a tombstone in deletion workflows?
options: A permanent backup of deleted bytes kept forever.
A marker that records deletion state and helps downstream systems propagate or verify removal.
A checksum mismatch discovered during replication.
A legal contract attached to a storage class.
questions.explanation: Tombstones preserve workflow state so deletion can propagate safely before physical purge.

questions.question: What is the biggest risk of deleting only the primary record and ignoring derived systems?
options: The storage bill drops too quickly.
Search, cache, or archive paths may continue serving supposedly deleted data.
Write amplification decreases unexpectedly.
The primary key becomes invalid for new inserts.
questions.explanation: Deletion semantics fail if stale derived paths still expose the data.

questions.question: Which metric best shows whether deletion workflows are healthy operationally?
options: Deletion completion latency and downstream propagation lag.
Only total number of application deployments.
Average blob size after compression.
Daily number of documentation edits.
questions.explanation: You need to know both how long deletes take and where they are stuck.

questions.question: Why are retention classes useful?
options: They align policy, cost, and compliance expectations across different data types.
They guarantee every query becomes faster over time.
They remove the need for audit logs.
They make backups unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Different data needs different retention and purge behavior, and classes make that explicit.

questions.question: What is the key trade-off of tombstone-first deletion?
options: It is safer and more auditable, but adds workflow complexity and temporary storage overhead.
It eliminates all asynchronous work immediately.
It makes legal holds impossible to implement.
It guarantees no cache will ever serve stale data.
questions.explanation: Tombstones improve safety, but they require state tracking and cleanup.

questions.question: How should a strong design handle legal hold?
options: Treat it as a centralized policy gate that can block or delay normal purge behavior.
Ignore it because it is outside the storage layer.
Apply it only after the physical purge is complete.
Use it only for relational databases.
questions.explanation: Legal hold must influence deletion decisions before destructive actions happen.

questions.question: Which interview answer sounds most senior?
options: We will run a nightly cron job to clean old rows and assume the rest catches up.
We will treat delete as a tracked workflow with policy checks, propagation, verification, and audit evidence.
We will keep deleted data indefinitely in search so restores are easier.
We will not discuss deletion because it is not on the critical path.
questions.explanation: Senior answers frame deletion as a correctness and compliance workflow, not background cleanup.
